text: The surgeon separates the mesocolon from the retroperitoneum by dissecting through the plane between these two structures in the retrocholic approach.
The surgeon navigates through an avascular plane to safeguard the ureter and gonadal vessel during the hemicolectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the area, encountering the duodenum positioned superiorly and medially. The dissection focuses on separating the duodenum from the mesocolon.
The surgeon exposes Gerota's fascia adjacent to the duodenum. After mobilizing the duodenum from the mesocolon, the pancreas becomes visible.
The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum-pancreatic complex from the mesocolon, revealing the colon reflected in the surgical field after mobilizing the plane.
The superior mesenteric vein is visible along with the draining vessels at the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon transitions to a supercolic approach, aiming to access the lesser sac by opening the gastrocolic omentum. Adhesions between the pancreas and stomach are carefully released, revealing a distinct plane between the mesocolon and the stomach.
The surgeon separates the mesocolic tissues, revealing the colonic and mesocolonic vasculature. Dissection is performed between the mesocolonic vasculature and the stomach tissue.
The surgeon navigates through the correct anatomical plane, revealing the C-loop of the duodenum while carefully preserving the colonic vasculature. The previously dissected plane becomes visible.
A gauze is placed to protect the underlying duodenum as the surgeon connects the infracolic and supracolic dissection planes.
The surgeon removes the final fibers of fatty tissue from the head of the pancreas, revealing the superior mesenteric vein and its draining veins.

The surgeon carefully dissects up to the root of the superior mesenteric vein, entering the tunnel of love. The middle colic vein is visible draining into the superior mesenteric vein, and the loop of end lay is observed.
The surgeon identifies a vein joining the loop of inlet, likely the right colic vein, and clips and divides it as it drains into the superior mesenteric vein.

After dissecting the area, the surgeon places a gauze to safeguard and identify the site later. The view shifts to the infracolic compartment where central vascular ligation begins. The superior mesenteric vein is clearly visible due to the lack of surrounding fat. The surgeon marks the left side of the superior mesenteric vein using a unipolar cautery device.

The surgeon begins dissection below the ileocolic vessels, applying traction to these vessels. Due to the presence of cecal growth, the fibro-fatty tissue around the ileocecal root is removed maximally. The surgeon marks a point at least four centimeters away from the root of the ileocecal vessels before proceeding with the removal of fibro-fatty tissues.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the superior mesenteric vein, entering the adventitial layer along the previously marked line. Small vessels are visible, requiring caution to prevent bleeding, as tissues to the left of the vein are separated into the specimen side.
The surgeon clips the ileocolic vein as it joins the superior mesenteric vein. The dissection continues along the ileocolic artery, extending to its root.

The surgeon approaches the origin of the ileocolic artery, clipping the vessels at this location. The previously dissected retromesenteric plane is visible, minimizing the risk of injuring nearby structures.
The surgeon dissects on the surface of the superior mesenteric artery, carefully identifying its branches while ensuring the metal part of the harmonic scalpel is not facing downward.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery and clips the small right colic artery arising from it, dividing the right colic artery during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic vein and the surrounding tissues from the root of the middle colic artery to its bifurcation into the right and left branches. The right branch of the middle colic artery is prepared for division due to the tumor located at the sacral area.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the proposed transaction area of the transverse colon. The lateral attachments of the ascending colon are divided, and the mesentery of the terminal ileum is dissected at least ten centimeters away from the ileocecal valve. Finally, the adhesions of the cecum to the abdominal wall are released.
The surgeon extracts the resected specimen through a small midline incision near the umbilicus. An extracorporeal ileotransverse anastomosis is performed, likely using hand-sewn techniques.
